layers.name: 01 · Channel Layer
layers.tagline: Where messages enter and leave — one adapter per platform

components.name: Channel Adapters
responsibilities: Maintain the connection to each platform (bot token, websocket session, or webhook endpoint)
Translate platform payloads into your internal message format — and back out again
Handle platform quirks: message length limits, markdown dialects, threading models, typing indicators

decisions.question: How does each platform deliver messages to you?
decisions.considerations: Webhooks need a public HTTPS endpoint (or a tunnel like ngrok/Cloudflare) — a real consideration on a home server. Websockets are outbound-only and firewall-friendly but need reconnect logic. Design the adapter interface so the transport is hidden from everything downstream.

decisions.question: One process per adapter, or one process for all?
decisions.considerations: A single asyncio process is simplest to start. Separate processes isolate crashes (a Discord library bug can't take down Slack) but add IPC complexity. A plugin interface keeps the door open for both.

components.name: Message Normalizer
responsibilities: Define ONE internal message format: sender identity, channel, conversation id, content blocks, attachments
Strip/convert platform formatting into something the agent core understands
Tag every message with provenance metadata (which channel, verified or not)

decisions.question: What does your canonical message schema look like?
decisions.considerations: This schema is the contract for your whole system — changing it later touches every adapter. Content blocks age better than a single text field once images and files show up. Pydantic gives you validation at the boundary, which is also a security control.

layers.name: 02 · Gateway & Routing
layers.tagline: The control plane — who gets in, and which agent they reach

components.name: Identity & Access Control
components.short: Who is this, and are they allowed?
responsibilities: Verify webhook signatures / bot token scopes so you know messages really came from the platform
Map platform identities (Discord id, phone number, WebEx email) to ONE internal user
Enforce allowlists: pairing flow for new users, tiered access for strangers vs. owner

decisions.question: Who is allowed to talk to the agent?
options: Pairing code flow for inviting users
decisions.considerations: This is the single highest-leverage security decision in the project. OpenClaw's open-by-default DMs were a repeated source of incidents. Start owner-only; design the identity model so multi-user is possible later without a rewrite.

decisions.question: How do you unify one human across channels?
decisions.considerations: Hyper-personalization requires knowing that discord:kevin and +1-555… are the same person, or memory fragments per channel. A linking table you edit by hand is fine at first — but decide early, because memory keys off this identity.

components.name: Message Queue & Dispatcher
responsibilities: Buffer inbound messages so a 30-second LLM call never blocks the webhook handler
Route each conversation to the right agent instance / session
Apply rate limits and debouncing (humans send 3 messages in a row; batch them)

decisions.question: How heavy should the queue be?
decisions.considerations: In-process queues are zero-ops but lose messages on crash. Redis is the sweet spot for a home server: survives restarts, doubles as a cache and session store. Full brokers earn their complexity only with multiple worker machines.

decisions.question: Serial or concurrent handling per conversation?
decisions.considerations: Serial-per-conversation avoids a whole class of race conditions and is what users intuitively expect. Concurrency across DIFFERENT conversations is where asyncio pays off.

layers.name: 03 · Agent Core
layers.tagline: The brain — sessions, persona, prompt assembly, memory

components.name: Session Manager
responsibilities: Create/load/expire sessions keyed by (user, channel, conversation)
Own the conversation history and its token budget
Decide when a session is 'stale' and should reset or summarize

decisions.question: What is a session, exactly?
decisions.considerations: If you text the agent and then open Discord, should it remember? Sharing sessions across channels is a signature 'personalized' feature — but it complicates concurrency and privacy. Hybrid (per-channel transcript + shared long-term memory) is a strong default.

decisions.question: How do you fit history into the context window?
decisions.considerations: Sliding windows silently forget commitments. Summarization preserves gist but costs tokens and can distort. Most real systems layer them: window + periodic summary + retrieval.

components.name: Persona & Personalization Engine
responsibilities: Store per-user persona config: tone, name, standing instructions, preferences
Merge persona + skills + memory into the system prompt deterministically
Allow multiple distinct agents (work agent, home agent) with isolated config

decisions.question: Where does personalization live?
decisions.considerations: Config files are auditable and version-controllable; learned memory is magical but can drift or be poisoned by injected instructions. If the agent can write its own memory, treat memory writes as a privileged tool call worth logging.

decisions.question: One agent with modes, or many isolated agents?
decisions.considerations: Isolation is a security boundary, not just organization: a work agent with Jira access shouldn't share a session space with a public Discord persona. This mirrors OpenClaw's multi-agent routing — decide the boundary early.

components.name: Prompt Assembler
responsibilities: Build the final request: system prompt, tool definitions, memory snippets, history, new message
Enforce token budgets per section (persona gets N, memory gets M…)
Clearly demarcate untrusted content (inbound messages, tool results) from trusted instructions

decisions.question: How do you mark untrusted content?
options: Spotlighting + instruction hierarchy in system prompt
decisions.considerations: No technique fully stops prompt injection — the model can't reliably distinguish data from instructions. But layering (roles + tagging + explicit hierarchy) meaningfully raises the bar, and the assembler is the one place you can enforce it consistently.

components.name: Memory System
responsibilities: Short-term: current session window
Long-term: durable facts and preferences per user
Retrieval: embed and search past conversations and documents (RAG)

decisions.question: Structured memory or embeddings-first?
decisions.considerations: Plain markdown memory files are shockingly effective, human-auditable, and debuggable — a great v1. Vector search adds fuzzy recall later. Whatever you choose, define who may write memory (user command? agent autonomously?) — that's a trust decision.

decisions.question: Which vector store, when you get there?
decisions.considerations: On a single home server, sqlite-vec or pgvector keeps your ops surface tiny. Dedicated stores matter at scale you likely won't hit for a personal framework.

layers.name: 04 · Capability Layer
layers.tagline: What the agent can DO — skills, MCP, and the blast-radius controls

components.name: Skill Registry
responsibilities: Discover, validate, and load skill definitions (instructions + optional tools)
Let each agent/user enable a curated subset — your 'selectable' requirement
Version skills and pin what production agents use

decisions.question: What IS a skill in your framework?
decisions.considerations: Instruction-only skills (like SKILL.md files) are safe to share and easy to write. The moment skills contain executable code you inherit the entire supply-chain problem — ClawHub audits found a significant share of malicious skills. Separating 'knowledge' (markdown) from 'capability' (vetted tools) is the cleanest security boundary in the whole design.

decisions.question: How are skills selected per request?
options: All enabled skills always in prompt
Model-driven selection (skill descriptions in prompt, bodies on demand)
decisions.considerations: Loading everything burns context. Progressive disclosure — descriptions always visible, full instructions loaded when relevant — is the pattern that scales.

components.name: MCP Client Manager
responsibilities: Spawn/connect to MCP servers (stdio child processes or remote HTTP)
Aggregate their tools into the model's tool list, namespaced per server
Lifecycle: tear down child processes cleanly, per-session or shared clients

decisions.question: stdio or HTTP transport for MCP servers?
decisions.considerations: stdio is simple and local-first but ties server lifetime to your process — orphaned children are a classic bug. HTTP allows one server shared across agents and remote hosting. Abstract the transport from day one.

decisions.question: Which MCP servers get attached to which agent?
decisions.considerations: A per-agent allowlist is your least-privilege mechanism: the public-facing agent gets read-only tools; only the owner's agent gets email-send or shell. Tool availability IS the permission model.

components.name: Tool Executor & Sandbox
responsibilities: Execute tool calls the model requests; return results into the conversation
Enforce approval gates for dangerous operations (shell, file writes, sending messages)
Contain execution: containers, restricted users, no default filesystem access

decisions.question: What requires human approval?
options: Allowlist of auto-approved tools, confirm the rest
decisions.considerations: This is your safety valve against both model mistakes and prompt injection: an injected instruction to 'email all my files' dies at the approval gate. Default-deny with a growing auto-approve list matches how trust actually develops.

decisions.question: How isolated is code/shell execution?
decisions.considerations: The agent will eventually run arbitrary-ish commands. A container boundary means a bad command or poisoned skill trashes a sandbox, not your server. Budget for this early — retrofitting isolation is painful.

components.name: LLM Router & Provider Adapters
responsibilities: Normalize provider APIs (Anthropic, OpenAI, local Ollama) behind one interface
Route by task: cheap/local model for triage & summarization, flagship for reasoning
Handle streaming, retries, rate limits, and prompt caching

decisions.question: Build the abstraction or use a library?
decisions.considerations: Hand-rolling teaches you the differences (tool-call formats differ meaningfully between providers). Libraries save time but add a dependency in your most security-sensitive data path. A thin adapter over 2 providers is a great learning project.

decisions.question: Where does cost control live?
decisions.considerations: A 24/7 agent with cron jobs can quietly burn real money. Instrument cost per session from day one — you can't control what you don't measure.

layers.name: 06 · Persistence & Cross-Cutting
layers.tagline: Everything that survives a restart — and everything watching

components.name: Data Stores
responsibilities: Session index + transcripts (append-only JSONL is a fine start)
User/identity tables and persona config
Vector store for retrieval memory

decisions.question: Files, SQLite, or Postgres?
decisions.considerations: Files are debuggable with cat and grep — genuinely valuable while learning. SQLite adds queryability with zero ops. Postgres earns its keep when you want pgvector and concurrent writers. You can graduate through all three.

components.name: Secrets & Config
responsibilities: Store API keys and bot tokens outside code and outside the agent's reach
Give the agent scoped, dedicated credentials — never your personal ones
Rotation story for when (not if) something leaks

decisions.question: How are secrets stored and injected?
decisions.considerations: The critical rule is architectural, not tooling: tool code receives credentials from the environment; the MODEL never sees them in context. If a secret appears in a prompt, assume it can be exfiltrated by injection.

components.name: Audit Log & Observability
components.short: What did the agent do, and why?
responsibilities: Append-only record of every tool call: who triggered it, inputs, outputs, approval status
Structured logging + metrics (latency, tokens, cost per session)
Health checks and alerting for the daemon

decisions.question: How tamper-resistant does the audit log need to be?
decisions.considerations: For a personal system, an append-only table you actually review beats a fancy chain you ignore. The habit matters more than the cryptography — build the review workflow, not just the log.

studyTopics.name: Async programming (asyncio)
studyTopics.summary: Event loops, coroutines, tasks, and cancellation. The gateway is fundamentally many concurrent I/O-bound connections.

studyTopics.name: Webhooks vs. websockets vs. polling
studyTopics.summary: Each platform delivers messages differently. Know the tradeoffs in latency, reliability, and firewall friendliness.

studyTopics.name: Message queues (Redis, RabbitMQ)
studyTopics.summary: Decouple 'message received' from 'agent responds' so one slow LLM call never blocks the pipeline.

studyTopics.name: State & persistence
studyTopics.summary: Session storage, transcripts, SQLite/Postgres basics, and schema design for per-user data.

studyTopics.name: Process management
studyTopics.summary: Daemons, systemd services, graceful shutdown, restart-on-crash. Daily life on a home server.

studyTopics.name: Context window management
studyTopics.summary: Token budgets, truncation, and summarization of long histories. The core constraint of everything.

studyTopics.name: Prompt assembly & system prompts
studyTopics.summary: How persona, skills, memory, and the user message get composed into each request.

studyTopics.name: Tool use / function calling
studyTopics.summary: How models request actions, and how you execute them and return results. The heart of 'skills'.

studyTopics.name: MCP (Model Context Protocol)
studyTopics.summary: Client vs. server roles, stdio vs. HTTP transports — the standard for pluggable tools.

studyTopics.name: Memory architectures
studyTopics.summary: Short-term (session), long-term (user profile), and retrieval (embeddings/RAG) — the 'hyper-personalized' part.

studyTopics.name: Model routing & cost control
studyTopics.summary: Cheap model for triage, expensive model for reasoning; caching; provider rate limits.

studyTopics.name: Prompt injection
studyTopics.summary: The #1 threat for agents reading inbound messages. Anyone who can DM the bot can attempt to steer it.

studyTopics.name: Authentication & identity
studyTopics.summary: Webhook signature verification, pairing/allowlisting, and mapping identities across channels.

studyTopics.name: Sandboxing & least privilege
studyTopics.summary: Containers, restricted shells, dedicated non-sensitive credentials. Never run the agent as root.

studyTopics.name: Secrets management
studyTopics.summary: Env vars vs. vaults, never committing keys, rotation strategy.

studyTopics.name: Supply chain risk
studyTopics.summary: Vetting third-party skills and dependencies — exactly how ClawHub got burned.

studyTopics.name: Confused deputy problem
studyTopics.summary: The agent has YOUR permissions but acts on OTHERS' input. This framing explains most agent security.

studyTopics.name: Audit logging
studyTopics.summary: Append-only logs of every tool call so you can reconstruct what the agent did and why.

messageLifecycle.detail: Tool calls → approval gate → sandboxed execution → results back to model
Format for channel → send → transcript + memory + audit log
